stories.headline: Artemis II Moon Mission Launches Successfully

articles.title: Artemis II Roars Into Space in Groundbreaking Moon Mission
articles.description: The Artemis II, NASA’s most powerful rocket ever, blasted off with four astronauts inside the Orion space capsule on Wednesday, beginning their journey to get humans back on the lunar surface for the first time in more than 50 years. While this mission will not include a lunar landing, it will chart a path for humans to return to the moon in 2028. NBC’s Tom Costello reports and former NASA astronaut Mike Massimino joins TODAY with analysis, saying “A new era has really started.”

articles.title: A look at Artemis II crew's "crazy first day" and what to expect next
articles.description: The mission commander of Artemis II, Reid Wiseman, said it would be a "crazy first day" and "it's 24 hours of intense work" as the lunar mission gets underway. Mark Strassmann looks at the critical decisions being made and what's next.

articles.title: Artemis II blasts off, beginning historic moon mission
articles.description: Four astronauts blasted off from the Kennedy Space Center on Wednesday to begin a historic space flight, which will loop around the far side of the moon. Artemis II is NASA's first crewed lunar mission since 1972. "CBS Evening News" anchor Tony Dokoupil has more.

articles.title: ‘Once in a Lifetime’: Passenger’s Artemis 2 View From Plane Goes Viral
articles.description: "Everyone on the plane was looking out the window trying to catch a glimpse of the rocket," the passenger noted in the viral post.

articles.title: Artemis II is in orbit - what happens next?
articles.description: What are the mission's four astronauts doing and when will they go to the Moon?

articles.title: WATCH:  Meet the 8-year-old who designed official moon mascot
articles.description: Out of more than 2,600 entries worldwide, Lucas Ye won the competition to create the "Zero Gravity Indicator" used on Artemis II.

articles.title: Artemis II crew wakes up in space for crucial 24 hours before heading to the moon
articles.description: The Artemis II crew will orbit the Earth for about 24 hours to inspect the Orion capsule before a decision is made to proceed toward the moon. CBS News' Cristian Benavides reports, and Les Johnson, a former chief technologist for NASA, joins CBS News with more details on the mission.

articles.title: How Mission Control will communicate with astronauts and monitor Artemis II flight
articles.description: For the rest of Artemis II's journey following its liftoff on Wednesday, the team at Mission Control in Houston will communicate with the astronauts, send commands and monitor the flight. Kris Van Cleave explains how it works.

articles.title: Here's where the Artemis II is right now
articles.description: The Artemis II crew is in space and is carrying out a full inspection of the Orion capsule that is required to get a green light to proceed toward the moon. CBS News' Mark Strassmann reports.

articles.title: Child goes viral for excited, blunt response to reporter's question about Artemis II moon mission
articles.description: A child went viral for his excited response about the Artemis II moon launch on CNN, catching the attention of NASA administrator Jared Isaacman, who said he would send NASA gear.

articles.title: How to track NASA’s Artemis II and Orion’s journey to the moon - The Washington Post
articles.description: How to track NASA’s Artemis II and Orion’s journey to the moon  The Washington Post

articles.title: Artemis II Astronauts' Menu For Moon Trip Leaves Everyone Making Same Joke
articles.description: Food items on the menu for the Artemis II crew include barbecued beef brisket, vegetable quiche, and broccoli au gratin.

analysis.summary: NASA's Artemis II mission launched on Wednesday with four astronauts aboard the Orion space capsule atop NASA's most powerful rocket, marking the first crewed lunar mission in over 50 years. The crew is conducting a 24-hour inspection of the Orion capsule in Earth orbit before proceeding toward the moon, which the spacecraft will loop around without landing. The mission has generated widespread public interest, including viral moments involving an aerial view of the rocket, a child's enthusiastic reaction, and an 8-year-old's mascot design.

analysis.summary: President Trump delivered a prime-time address declaring military progress in the ongoing US-Israeli war against Iran, vowing to hit Iran 'extremely hard' over the next two to three weeks and threatening to bring the country 'back to the Stone Age.' The speech triggered sharp market declines, with Dow futures falling roughly 600 points and oil prices surging past $110, while Iran responded with fresh missile attacks on Israel and Gulf states and international allies expressed concern or dissent.

A separate GOP-only bill to fund border security operations is expected to follow.

analysis.summary: Tiger Woods was arrested for driving under the influence following a rollover car crash in Florida on March 27, with police body camera footage subsequently released showing his condition and interactions with deputies at the scene. Woods was handcuffed, had pills found in his pocket, admitted to taking 'a few pills,' and failed a sobriety test, before later pleading not guilty and announcing he would seek treatment. The arrest has prompted public criticism and raised concerns about financial consequences for his business ventures.

articles.title: Artemis II Roars Into Space in Groundbreaking Moon Mission
articles.description: The Artemis II, NASA’s most powerful rocket ever, blasted off with four astronauts inside the Orion space capsule on Wednesday, beginning their journey to get humans back on the lunar surface for the first time in more than 50 years. While this mission will not include a lunar landing, it will chart a path for humans to return to the moon in 2028. NBC’s Tom Costello reports and former NASA astronaut Mike Massimino joins TODAY with analysis, saying “A new era has really started.”

articles.title: A look at Artemis II crew's "crazy first day" and what to expect next
articles.description: The mission commander of Artemis II, Reid Wiseman, said it would be a "crazy first day" and "it's 24 hours of intense work" as the lunar mission gets underway. Mark Strassmann looks at the critical decisions being made and what's next.

articles.title: Artemis II blasts off, beginning historic moon mission
articles.description: Four astronauts blasted off from the Kennedy Space Center on Wednesday to begin a historic space flight, which will loop around the far side of the moon. Artemis II is NASA's first crewed lunar mission since 1972. "CBS Evening News" anchor Tony Dokoupil has more.

articles.title: ‘Once in a Lifetime’: Passenger’s Artemis 2 View From Plane Goes Viral
articles.description: "Everyone on the plane was looking out the window trying to catch a glimpse of the rocket," the passenger noted in the viral post.

articles.title: Artemis II is in orbit - what happens next?
articles.description: What are the mission's four astronauts doing and when will they go to the Moon?

articles.title: WATCH:  Meet the 8-year-old who designed official moon mascot
articles.description: Out of more than 2,600 entries worldwide, Lucas Ye won the competition to create the "Zero Gravity Indicator" used on Artemis II.

articles.title: Artemis II crew wakes up in space for crucial 24 hours before heading to the moon
articles.description: The Artemis II crew will orbit the Earth for about 24 hours to inspect the Orion capsule before a decision is made to proceed toward the moon. CBS News' Cristian Benavides reports, and Les Johnson, a former chief technologist for NASA, joins CBS News with more details on the mission.

articles.title: How Mission Control will communicate with astronauts and monitor Artemis II flight
articles.description: For the rest of Artemis II's journey following its liftoff on Wednesday, the team at Mission Control in Houston will communicate with the astronauts, send commands and monitor the flight. Kris Van Cleave explains how it works.

articles.title: Here's where the Artemis II is right now
articles.description: The Artemis II crew is in space and is carrying out a full inspection of the Orion capsule that is required to get a green light to proceed toward the moon. CBS News' Mark Strassmann reports.

articles.title: Child goes viral for excited, blunt response to reporter's question about Artemis II moon mission
articles.description: A child went viral for his excited response about the Artemis II moon launch on CNN, catching the attention of NASA administrator Jared Isaacman, who said he would send NASA gear.

articles.description: Food items on the menu for the Artemis II crew include barbecued beef brisket, vegetable quiche, and broccoli au gratin.

analysis.summary: NASA's Artemis II mission launched on Wednesday carrying four astronauts aboard the Orion spacecraft atop NASA's most powerful rocket, marking the first crewed lunar mission in over 50 years. The mission will loop around the far side of the moon without landing, serving as a pathfinder for a future lunar landing targeted for 2028. Coverage spans the historic launch itself, a reported toilet malfunction hours after liftoff, the crew's 24-hour Earth orbit inspection period, and various viral human-interest stories surrounding the mission.

analysis.summary: The United States and Israel are engaged in an ongoing military conflict with Iran, now in its fifth week, involving airstrikes on hundreds of targets, missile exchanges, and threats of further escalation. President Trump delivered a national address claiming the mission is proceeding according to plan and could end within weeks, while Iran responded defiantly with missile launches and fresh attacks. The conflict has prompted international reactions ranging from European criticism of U.S. strategy to GCC appeals to the UN, amid concerns about the Strait of Hormuz and regional stability.

articles.title: Artemis II Roars Into Space in Groundbreaking Moon Mission
articles.description: The Artemis II, NASA’s most powerful rocket ever, blasted off with four astronauts inside the Orion space capsule on Wednesday, beginning their journey to get humans back on the lunar surface for the first time in more than 50 years. While this mission will not include a lunar landing, it will chart a path for humans to return to the moon in 2028. NBC’s Tom Costello reports and former NASA astronaut Mike Massimino joins TODAY with analysis, saying “A new era has really started.”

articles.title: Artemis II commander enters tablet PIN on launch livestream, leaving mankind stunned

Viewers wasted no time trying to guess astronaut Reid Wiseman’s code after he unlocked his device 19 minutes before Wednesday night’s launch. Artemis II Commander Reid Wiseman...

articles.title: The Artemis II crew is in space. Here's what's next.

articles.title: Artemis II Roars Into Space in Groundbreaking Moon Mission
articles.description: The Artemis II, NASA’s most powerful rocket ever, blasted off with four astronauts inside the Orion space capsule on Wednesday, beginning their journey to get humans back on the lunar surface for the first time in more than 50 years. While this mission will not include a lunar landing, it will chart a path for humans to return to the moon in 2028. NBC’s Tom Costello reports and former NASA astronaut Mike Massimino joins TODAY with analysis, saying “A new era has really started.”

articles.title: A look at Artemis II crew's "crazy first day" and what to expect next

analysis.summary: NASA's Artemis II mission successfully launched four astronauts aboard the Orion capsule on an SLS rocket from Kennedy Space Center, marking humanity's first crewed lunar mission in over 50 years. The crew entered orbit and began a 24-hour inspection of the Orion capsule before a decision would be made to proceed toward the moon. The mission generated widespread public interest, spawning numerous viral moments and human interest stories alongside serious mission coverage.

analysis.summary: President Trump delivered a prime-time national address defending continued U.S. military action against Iran, declaring objectives are 'nearing completion' while vowing to hit Iran 'extremely hard' over the next two to three weeks. The speech triggered sharp negative reactions from financial markets, with oil prices surging and stocks falling, while drawing criticism from allies, Democrats, and some Republicans for its lack of clarity. Iran responded with fresh missile attacks on Israel and Gulf states, and the conflict has entered its fifth week.

While this mission will not include a lunar landing, it will chart a path for humans to return to the moon in 2028. NBC’s Tom Costello reports and former NASA astronaut Mike Massimino joins TODAY with analysis, saying “A new era has really started.”
